coredata.dc:title: The antioxidant ability of Rosa sterilis carbon dots on oils
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Yanfei
Chen, Hongqin
Li, Yuanlin
Huang, Yongguang
coredata.dc:description: Quality of edible oils is a critical component of food safety. The antioxidant properties of carbon dots (CDs) have been increasingly investigated. In this study, Rosa sterilis carbon dots (J-CDs) were synthesized using Rosa sterilis as the carbon source via a one-step hydrothermal method. The structural characteristics and morphology of J-CDs were characterized using techniques such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). We have further explored the patterns of changes in oxidation parameters and fatty acid composition during the process of delaying lipid oxidation with different dosages of J-CDs were investigated. The results showed that the average particle size of the J-CDs was 2.75 nm, with an approximately spherical morphology. The particles exhibited a graphite-like carbon structure and were enriched with oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on their surfaces. J-CDs exhibited significant scavenging effects on DPPH and ABTS radicals, with Effective Concentration 50 (EC50) values of 29.07 μg/mL and 8.13 μg/mL, respectively, and demonstrated a notable capacity for reducing ferric ions. Furthermore, as the dosage of J-CDs increased to 0.06%, the acid value (AV) decreased to 1.00 ± 0.08 mg/g, the peroxide value (PV) dropped to 3.74 ± 0.24 mmol/kg, the p-anisidine value (PAV) reduced to 102, and the total oxidation value (TOTOX) declined to 110. These findings suggested that the addition of J-CDs effectively inhibited the oxidation of sunflower oil.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass nanomaterials
Free radical scavenging
Antioxidant
Oil
Food safety

Effect of J-CDs on the oxidative stability of oils
Preparation of the samples of J-CDs-added oils
Determination of AV, PV, PAV, and TOTOX
Analysis of antioxidant properties of J-CDs
Effect of J-CDs on the oxidation of oils during the high-temperature heating process
Results of AV, PV, PAV, and TOTOX determination

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The support of the current project was provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 32060534 and 32360571), Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (Natural Science) (Grant No. ZK [2023] 118, 166; ZK[2024] Major Project 016), Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology Support Project (Grant No. [2023] 343), Guizhou Provincial Department of Information Industry Project (Grant No. [2020]198), Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Guizhou Province (Qianke Zhongyindi [2019]4019), General horizontal project (Grant No. K22-0115-014, Qianrenlingfa [2020]8) and Guizhou University Natural Science Special Scientific Research Fund Project (X2021327).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of J-CDs. (a) TEM imaging. (b) Size distribution of J-CDs.
XPS characterization of J-CDs. (a) XPS total spectrum of J-CDs. (b) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s. (c) High-resolution XPS spectra of O1s. (d) High-resolution XPS spectra of N1s.

The scavenging results of VC and J-CDs on DPPH radicals. (a) The scavenging rate spectrum of VC on DPPH radicals. (b) The scavenging rate curve of VC on DPPH radicals. (c) Scavenging rate curve of J-CDs on DPPH radicals. (d) The scavenging rate curve of J-CDs on DPPH radicals. (e) Schematic diagram of the scavenging mechanism of J-CDs on DPPH radicals.

The scavenging results of VC and J-CDs on ABTS radicals. (a) The scavenging rate spectrum of VC on ABTS radicals. (b) The scavenging rate curve of VC on ABTS radicals. (c) The scavenging rate spectrum of J-CDs on ABTS radicals. (d) The scavenging rate curve of J-CDs on ABTS radicals. (e) Schematic diagram of the scavenging mechanism of J-CDs on ABTS radicals.
The oxidation results of oils at 170 °C. (a) Variation of AV. (b) Variation of PV. (c) Variation of PAV. (d) Variation of TOTOX.
At 170 °C, sunflower oil added with varying amounts of J-CDs caused changes in the fatty acid composition. (a) control. (b) 0.02%. (c) 0.04%. (d) 0.06%. (e) 0.08%.

head.ce:title: The antioxidant ability of Rosa sterilis carbon dots on oils

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering and Biological Pharmacy of Guizhou Province, College of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang, Guizhou, 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering and Biological Pharmacy of Guizhou Province
College of Liquor and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering and Biological Pharmacy of Guizhou Province, College of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang, Guizhou, 550025, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quality of edible oils is a critical component of food safety. The antioxidant properties of carbon dots (CDs) have been increasingly investigated. In this study, Rosa sterilis carbon dots (J-CDs) were synthesized using Rosa sterilis as the carbon source via a one-step hydrothermal method. The structural characteristics and morphology of J-CDs were characterized using techniques such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). We have further explored the patterns of changes in oxidation parameters and fatty acid composition during the process of delaying lipid oxidation with different dosages of J-CDs were investigated. The results showed that the average particle size of the J-CDs was 2.75 nm, with an approximately spherical morphology. The particles exhibited a graphite-like carbon structure and were enriched with oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on their surfaces. J-CDs exhibited significant scavenging effects on DPPH and ABTS radicals, with Effective Concentration 50 (EC50) values of 29.07 μg/mL and 8.13 μg/mL, respectively, and demonstrated a notable capacity for reducing ferric ions. Furthermore, as the dosage of J-CDs increased to 0.06%, the acid value (AV) decreased to 1.00 ± 0.08 mg/g, the peroxide value (PV) dropped to 3.74 ± 0.24 mmol/kg, the p-anisidine value (PAV) reduced to 102, and the total oxidation value (TOTOX) declined to 110. These findings suggested that the addition of J-CDs effectively inhibited the oxidation of sunflower oil.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging class of nanomaterials primarily composed of carbon that may also contain heteroatoms such as oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur, along with various surface functional groups (Kirubanithy et al., 2024). In recent years, as nanotechnology and biomaterials science have advanced, the antioxidant properties of CDs have become increasingly apparent (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023). Carbon nanomaterials have been extensively studied and have great application prospects in the field of antioxidants owing to their large specific surface area, excellent hydrogen-donating and electron-transfer capabilities, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity (Murugan et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024; Yu et al., 2024; Zheng et al., 2023). Antioxidant activity refers to the ability of a substance to scavenge or inhibit free radicals and other oxidative substances, thereby preventing or slowing down oxidation reactions (Lopez et al., 2024; Zeng et al., 2024). Some CDs, owing to their abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups, can function as efficient electron or hydrogen donors in antioxidant reactions, thereby neutralizing free radicals and exhibiting pronounced antioxidant activities (Gao et al., 2023). To date, several researchers have been able to utilize natural and sustainable materials including bananas (Wu et al., 2023), sugarcane bagasse (Akash et al., 2024), elephant manure (Seesuea et al., 2024), and gardenia seeds (Tsai et al., 2023) as precursors for CDs synthesis. These previous studies have demonstrated that the resulting CDs exhibit significant antioxidant activity. Therefore, the excellent antioxidant properties of CDs are closely related to their natural structure and chemical activity (Guo et al., 2024; Sul et al., 2023a; Zhao et al., 2023).

Edible oils are indispensable for human life and provide energy and the necessary fatty acids (Ma et al., 2023). Nevertheless, they are vulnerable to oxidation via several mechanisms. When edible oils are oxidized, essential nutrients deteriorate, and toxic substances are produced, causing health problems for consumers (Anconi et al., 2022). Therefore, delaying the oxidation of edible oils is essential to ensure their quality and safety (Wang et al., 2023). Several approaches have been employed to prevent oxidation of edible oils. These methods include reducing the content of polyunsaturated fatty acids and partially hydrogenated fats and removing oxygen from the products. Among these strategies, the addition of antioxidants is considered one of the most effective means of enhancing the oxidative stability of edible oil (Wu et al., 2022). However, antioxidants can be unstable in oxygen, degrade before reaching their target, or pose health risks, which restricts their application (Jiang et al., 2024). In this context, nanomaterials offer significant advantages including enhanced catalytic stability, improved tolerance to environmental conditions, reduced costs, simplified preparation processes, and superior surface modifiability (Baschieri & Amorati, 2021).

In this study, Rosa sterilis was used as the raw material in order to prepare Rosa sterilis carbon dots (J-CDs). Rosa sterilis is a plant abundant in antioxidant compounds, and its fruits and leaves contain significant amounts of vitamin C (VC), vitamin E (VE), flavonoids, and other antioxidants. Therefore, the preparation of CDs from Rosa sterilis may facilitate the retention of their natural antioxidant components within the framework of CDs during carbonization, thereby endowing the CDs with an enhanced antioxidant activity. J-CDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. The scavenging abilities of J-CDs against DPPH and ABTS radicals and their ability to reduce iron ions have been systematically investigated. In addition, the potential of J-CDs to inhibit lipid oxidation was evaluated.

The Rosa sterilis was sourced from a local market in Guiyang City, Guizhou Province. All solvents utilized in the experiments were of analytical grade, which were employed directly without the need for further purification or treatment. Ultrahigh purity water was consistently used for all experimental procedures throughout the entire research process.

According to the method of Sul et al. (Sul et al., 2023a), 25 mL of Rosa sterilis juice was sonicated for 15 min in a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave, then heated at 180 °C for 8 h and cooled. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm (4440 g) for 10 min (20 °C), filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. The resulting brown powder was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C for further use and analysis.

A JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope was used to inspect the J-CDs solutions prepared at a concentration of 1 mg/mL. The sample solution was sonicated for 10 min for complete dispersion. An ultrathin carbon film copper mesh was placed in a glass dish lined with filter paper. A pipette gun was used to add 10 μL of J-CDs solution vertically onto the copper mesh, which was then dried with an infrared lamp. The operation was repeated to 4–5 times. The JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope captured and analyzed the size and shape of the J-CDs.

The J-CDs powder (1 mg) was thoroughly mixed with dried potassium bromide (KBr) powder in a ratio of 1:100 and subsequently pressed into a transparent thin wafer. The wafer was analyzed using a Nicolet iS 10 FTIR spectrometer. The instrument parameters were set as follows: wavelength range of 4000–500 cm−1, step width of 2 cm−1, and resolution of 4 cm−1, and the results were recorded as the transmittance. Background subtraction was performed after measurement.

EscaLab250Xi XPS analysis was used to analyze the surface chemistry and elemental content of the J-CDs. Freeze-dried J-CDs powder was prepared, the surfaces were cleaned and sputtered with an ion or electron beam, the samples were aligned for spectral correction, and the data were collected.

Using a known method (Zhao et al., 2022). J-CDs DPPH radical scavenging ability was tested by mixing 2 mL DPPH ethanol solution with J-CDs (0–128 μg/mL) and VC (0–20 μg/mL), then measuring absorbance at 517 nm after a 20-min dark incubation. Equation (1) illustrates the DPPH scavenging capacity of J-CDs.
(1)
D P P H
s c a v e n g i n g
c a p a c i t y
( % )
=
(
1 ‐
A j
A 0
)
× 100 %
where A
j and A
0 represent the absorbance of the DPPH solution in the presence and absence of J-CDs, respectively, using VC as a benchmark antioxidant. The EC50 value reflects the effectiveness of the J-CDs in scavenging DPPH radicals.

The method described by Zhou et al. was followed with some slight modifications in order to determine the ABTS radical-scavenging activity of the J-CDs (Zhou et al., 2023). The ABTS radical stock solution was diluted with anhydrous ethanol to achieve an absorbance of 0.90 ± 0.02 at 734 nm. Four milliliters of the diluted solution were mixed with 1 mL of VC solution at different concentrations (0–4.5 μg/mL) and 1 mL of J-CDs solution at different concentrations (0–20 μg/mL), making the total reaction volume 5 mL. The mixture was vortexed at room temperature (20 °C) and incubated in the dark for 30 min. The absorbance was measured at 734 nm. The percentage of ABTS radical scavenging ability of J-CDs was calculated using formula (2).
(2)
A B T S
s c a v e n g i n g
c a p a c i t y
( % )
=
(
1 ‐
A j
A 0
)
× 100 %

Equation (2) uses VC as the benchmark antioxidant, where A
j and A
0 are the absorbances of the ABTS solution with and without J-CDs, respectively. The EC50 value indicates the ABTS scavenging efficacy of the J-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the ferric reducing antioxidant power
ce:section.ce:para: The ferric reducing antioxidant power (FRAP) of the J-CDs was confirmed by following a procedure similar to that reported in the literature (Wen et al., 2023a). The VC and J-CDs solutions were prepared at concentrations of 0.00, 0.10, 0.20, 0.50, 1.00, and 2.00 mg/mL. Then, 4.90 mL the TPTZ solution was added, shaken for 10 s, and left in the dark for 30 min before measuring the absorbance at 593 nm. Concentrations were plotted on the y-axis versus absorbance on the x-axis, and each experiment was conducted in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of J-CDs on the oxidative stability of oils
Preparation of the samples of J-CDs-added oils
ce:section.ce:para: J-CDs oil samples were prepared according to a previously published method with minor modifications (Zeng et al., 2024). Different concentrations of J-CDs (0%, 0.02%, 0.04%, 0.06%, 0.08% w/w) were added to sunflower oil with 5 groups, respectively. The mixtures were subjected to ultrasonication for 20 min to achieve uniform dissolution, and then heated in an oil bath at 170 °C for 5 h. The samples were collected hourly to measure physicochemical indicators, in order to analyze the impact of J-CDs on the degree of thermal oxidation of sunflower oil. After 5 h, the reaction was terminated and changes in the fatty acid composition were analyzed using GC-MS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AV, PV, PAV, and TOTOX
ce:section.ce:para:
Determination was conducted according to a previously described method with minor modifications (GB 5009.229-2016, 2016). A 0.25 g aliquot of sunflower seed oil was accurately pipetted into a clean container, followed by the addition of 5 mL of an ether-isopropanol (1:1, v/v) solution and three drops of phenolphthalein indicator (10 mg/mL, dissolved in 95% ethanol). The mixture was thoroughly stirred and then titrated with a standardized 0.1 mol/L sodium hydroxide solution until a faint pink color persisted for at least 15 s. The titrant volume was recorded. The acid value of the sample was also calculated using Equation (3).
(3)
A V
(
mg / g
)
=
(
V ‐
V 0
)
× c × M
m
where m is the sunflower oil mass (g), V is the volume of NaOH (mL), V
0 is the volume of NaOH in the blank (mL), c is the NaOH concentration (mol/L), and M is the molar mass of NaOH (g/mol).

The PV was determined in accordance with the national standard GB 5009.227–2023 (GB 5009.227-2023, 2023). Specifically, 0.2 g of sunflower seed oil was accurately weighed and mixed with 3 mL acetic acid-chloroform (3:2, v/v) solution. Then, 500 μL of saturated potassium iodide solution (1.4 g/mL) was added. The mixture was incubated in the dark at 25 °C for 3 min. Subsequently, 10 mL of ultrapure water was added, and the liberated iodine was titrated with a standardized 0.01 mol/L sodium thiosulfate solution until a light-yellow color appeared. At this point, 500 μL of starch indicator (10 mg/mL) was added, and the mixture was thoroughly shaken. The titration was continued until the blue-purple color disappeared, and the volume consumed was recorded. A blank control without sunflower seed oil was also prepared and titrated under identical conditions. The peroxide value of the oil was calculated using Equation (4).
(4)
P V
(
mmol kg
)
=
(
V −
V 0
)
× c × 500
m
where V is the volume of Na2S2O3 (mL), V
0 is the blank volume (mL), m is the mass of sunflower oil (g), c is the Na2S2O3 concentration (mol/L), and 500 is the conversion factor.

The PAV and TOTOX values were determined according to national standard GB/T 24304–2009 (GB/T 24304-2009, 2009). Specifically, 0.5 mL of sunflower seed oil (0.04 g/mL, dissolved in iso-octane (0.1 mL) was mixed with p-anisidine reagent (2.5 mL) dissolved in acetic acid) and thoroughly stirred. The mixture was incubated at 25 °C for 8 min, and the absorbance at 350 nm was measured and recorded as A
1 . For the blank control, 0.1 mL an acetic acid solution was used instead of the p-anisidine reagent following the same procedure. The absorbance was measured in the same manner and recorded as A
2 . Isooctane was used as the blank solvent, and its absorbance was recorded as A
0 . The p-anisidine content was calculated using Equation (5).
(5)
P A V =
(
A 1
−
A 2
−
A 0
)
× c × V × 1.2
m
× 100
where c is the mass concentration of the oil sample solution (g/mL), V is the volume of the oil sample solution (mL), m is the mass of the oil sample (g), and 1.2 is the conversion factor.

TOTOX was determined using the following Equation (6).
(6)
TOTOX = 2 PV + PAV
where PV is the peroxide, PAV is the p-anisidine value, and TOTOX is the total oxidation value.
A colorimeter, following the CIE standards, was used to measure the color of the oil samples and accurately assess their brightness (L∗), red-green (a∗), and yellow-blue (b∗) attributes.

The oxidation products of grease were analyzed using GC-MS in conjunction with J-CDs, and the inhibitory effect of J-CDs on grease oxidation was validated according to GB 5009.168–2016 (GB 5009.168–2016), the national standard for determining fatty acids in food. For sample methyl esterification, take 0.1 g of oil in a centrifuge tube, add 2 mL of a petroleum ether-ether mixture (2:1 v/v), dissolve by shaking, then add 1 mL of 2% potassium hydroxide in methanol and vortex. Incubate in a 40 °C water bath for 1 h. After the reaction, the mixture was cooled to room temperature (20 °C) and 10 mL of distilled water was added. After standing for 30–60 min, the supernatant was carefully transferred to a gas-phase vial for subsequent measurements.

In chromatographic analysis, a FFAP quartz capillary column (30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.3 μm) was utilized with an initial temperature of 50 °C for 5 min, followed by an increase to 230 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min, maintained for 20 min. A hydrogen FID detector set at 270 °C and an inlet temperature of 250 °C were applied. Fatty acids including palmitic (C16:0), stearic (C18:0), oleic (C18:1), linoleic (C18:2), linolenic (C18:3), and behenic (C22:0) acids were quantified using an external standard.

The oil samples were measured thrice for accuracy. The randomized design ensured the objectivity and reproducibility of the study. Data were evaluated using one-way (ANOVA) and Tukey's test at a 95% confidence level to determine group mean differences. The results are presented as mean ± standard deviation of three replicates, and the data analysis is performed by Origin 2024 software.

The morphology and size of the J-CDs were characterized using TEM. The TEM results (Fig. 1 a) showed that at a scale resolution of 20 nm, the J-CDs exhibited approximately spherical and elliptical morphologies, appearing as monodisperse nanoparticles (Liu et al., 2020). They were well dispersed in aqueous solution with uniform particle distribution, and no aggregation was observed. Furthermore, the HRTEM images revealed a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which is consistent with the (100) plane of graphite (Fig. 1 inset). This indicates that J-CDs possess a graphitic carbon framework structure. Additionally, Fig. 1b shows that the particle size distribution of J-CDs ranges from 1.75 to 4.0 nm, with an average size of approximately 2.75 nm, indicating that the prepared CDs have a relatively uniform particle size. This result preliminarily demonstrates that nanoscale carbon particles were successfully formed from Rosa sterilis via a one-step hydrothermal process, and their morphology and size fully conform to the definition CDs (Crista et al., 2020; Shan et al., 2022).

The surface functional groups of J-CDs were characterized using FTIR within the range of 500–4000 cm−1 (see Fig. 2 ). The results indicated that J-CDs exhibited a broad band in the range of 3200–3500 cm−1, which is likely attributed to the characteristic absorption peaks of N-H and O-H bonds, confirming their strong polarity (Xing et al., 2023). Additionally, the characteristic peaks observed at 1352, 1595, and 1664 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration frequencies of C-O, C=C, and C=O bonds, respectively. Meanwhile, the absorption bands at 768, 1195, and 2815 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-H bonds (Gao et al., 2020; Rajendran et al., 2021). The FTIR spectra further confirmed the chemical structure of J-CDs, indicating the presence of functional groups containing C, N, and O atoms on the J-CDs backbone. These functional groups primarily include oxygen-rich moieties such as phenolic hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. This result indicates that the J-CDs synthesized by the hydrothermal method possess hydrophilic functional groups, enabling them to form complexes with other ions or molecules.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the XPS full spectrum of J-CDs reveals characteristic peaks for the elements C (285 eV), N (400 eV), and O (532 eV), with corresponding atomic percentages of 71.81%, 1.32%, and 26.87%, respectively (Fig. 3 inset). These findings suggest that Rosa sterilis, which are rich in organic substances such as polysaccharides, cellulose, and pectin, offer potential advantages as raw materials for synthesizing CDs. The organic components were converted into carbon materials during the hydrothermal carbonization process, thereby providing an abundant carbon source for the synthesis of J-CDs (Kang et al., 2020). In the high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b), three peaks were observed at 284.3, 285.6, and 287.6 eV, which were attributed to C-C/C=C, C=O, and C-O bonds, respectively (Chung, LunAng, & Soraya, 2021; Liu et al., 2023). Additionally, in the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 3c), two characteristic peaks were observed at 531.0 and 532.2 eV, which were attributed to C=O and C-O bonds, respectively (Wei et al., 2023). The XPS spectrum of N1s shows two peak signals at 399.7 and 402.0 eV (Fig. 3d), corresponding to pyrrolic nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen, respectively (Wu Hao et al., 2020). The XPS spectra further corroborated the FTIR results. Collectively, these findings indicate that the original juice of Rosa sterilis, through a one-step hydrothermal process, endows the J-CDs with the corresponding characteristic functional groups and forms a graphite-like structure, thereby successfully transforming Rosa sterilis juice into nanoscale carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of antioxidant properties of J-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The DPPH radical exhibited a distinct absorption peak at a wavelength of 517 nm, as shown in Fig. 4 a. With VC as the standard antioxidant, the experimentally measured EC50 for DPPH free radicals was 5.43 μg/mL (Fig. 4b). Furthermore, Fig. 4c and d demonstrate that as the concentration of the J-CDs increases from 0 μg/mL to 64 μg/mL, the scavenging rate of DPPH radicals by the J-CDs rises from 6.82% to 84.92%. Following fitting and computational analysis, the EC50 of the J-CDs for scavenging the DPPH radical was determined to be 29.07 μg/mL. Recent studies have reported that carbon dots synthesized from Azadirachta indica demonstrated a EC50 of 13.87 μg/mL in scavenging DPPH free radicals (Gedda et al., 2023). This finding underscores the pronounced efficacy of the J-CDs in neutralizing DPPH radicals. The antioxidant activity of the J-CDs is due to surface oxygenation functional groups, which can scavenge and quench DPPH radicals by transferring H• from -OH to DPPH-H on the J-CDs (Sul et al., 2023b). Consequently, it is plausible that the J-CDs neutralize free radicals via their surface oxygen-containing functional groups, thereby forming stable complexes with these radicals.

Fig. 5 illustrates the ABTS radical scavenging efficiency of the J-CDs. By comparing the scavenging capacities of VC and J-CDs at various concentrations, the EC50 values for ABTS radicals were determined to be 1.94 μg/mL for VC and 8.13 μg/mL for J-CDs. The EC50 results indicate that J-CDs exhibit a robust scavenging effect on ABTS radicals, with a scavenging efficiency only marginally lower than that of VC. Typically, ABTS radicals exhibit strong absorption at 734 nm. As the concentration of J-CDs increases from 0 μg/mL to 20 μg/mL, the scavenging efficiency for ABTS radicals rises from 22.06% to 94.03%, and the solution color changes from blue-green to light yellow (Fig. 5d inset). It has been elaborated that the main way of scavenging radicals by the phenolic hydroxyl groups was through hydrogen atom transfer (ArOH + R•→ArO•+RH) and the single electron transfer (ArOH + R•→ArOH+•+R−) (Luo et al., 2024). The excellent ABTS⁺ radical scavenging potential of J-CDs may be attributed to the involvement of the phenolic hydroxyl groups on their surface in the removal of radicals, thereby forming stable complexes in the ABTS assay.

The original Rosa sterilis juice contains several active components, and the magnitude of its reducing power is significantly correlated with its antioxidant capacity. In this experiment, VC was used as a control to assess the reducing power of the prepared J-CDs using a FRAP assay. Specifically, higher absorbance values indicated stronger reducing power. As shown in Fig. 6 , the ferric reducing power of both VC and J-CDs gradually increased as the concentration increased from 0 mg/mL to 2.0 mg/mL. According to the literature, green tea has been used to prepare carbon dots, and their antioxidant properties have been investigated through the iron reduction ability. The antioxidant properties were investigated through the iron reduction. It was found that when the concentration of carbon dots increased from 0 to 10 μg/mL, the absorbance value increased from 0.03 to 0.44 (Wen et al., 2023b). It can be seen that the precursors of J-CDs contain a variety of surface groups, which endow J-CDs with a certain ability to reduce iron ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of J-CDs on the oxidation of oils during the high-temperature heating process
Results of AV, PV, PAV, and TOTOX determination
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the impact of J-CDs antioxidant properties on sunflower oil under high temperature conditions, we heated sunflower oil spiked with various concentrations of J-CDs at high temperatures to simulate the oxidation process of oil. Then, the extent of oil oxidation was assessed by measuring its AV, PV, PAV, and TOTOX. This allowed us to determine the impact of J-CDs on delaying the oil change process.

As shown in Fig. 7 a–b, the addition of different concentrations of J-CDs significantly affected the AV and PV of sunflower oil. AV and PV are critical indicators of the extent of lipid deterioration and oxidation. Lower AV and PV values indicate fresher oil samples (Zabeti et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2021). In this study, sunflower oil was subjected to air oxidation at 170 °C for 5 h. The AV and PV of all of the oil samples significantly increased. This increase was primarily attributed to the hydrolysis and thermal oxidation of the triglycerides in the oil at 170 °C, leading to the formation of free fatty acids (FFA). These FFA subsequently react with oxygen in air, resulting in the generation of a series of peroxides. Among the samples, the AV of the control group increased the most significantly (Fig. 7a), whereas that of the groups containing J-CDs generally exhibited a downward trend. It is noteworthy that as the concentration of J-CDs increases from 0% to 0.06%, the AV after 5 h decreases from 2.23 ± 0.21 mg/g to 1.00 ± 0.08 mg/g. This indicates that J-CDs can effectively inhibit the increase in AV during high-temperature air oxidation of sunflower oil. This may be related to the ability of the J-CDs to inhibit the oxidation and hydrolysis of oils. Studies have indicated that the primary mechanism for the formation of lipid oxidation products involves the removal of hydrogen atoms from allylic positions, forming carbon-centered alkyl radicals in the initial step. These radicals are generated when they react with the atmospheric oxygen. J-CDs can neutralize these alkyl radicals through hydrogen proton transfer, thereby inhibiting lipid oxidation (Ampem et al., 2024). Further analysis of the PV revealed that the control group exhibited the highest PV, increasing to 6.31 ± 0.34 mmol/kg. Notably, when the concentration of J-CDs was increased from 0% to 0.06%, the PV after 5 h decreased from 6.31 ± 0.34 mmol/kg to 3.74 ± 0.24 mmol/kg. As indicated in literature, phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs exhibit excellent free-radical scavenging effects. According to the FTIR spectrum, J-CDs show a broad O-H characteristic peak in the range of 3200–3500 cm−1, which suggests that the phenolic hydroxyl groups are abundant on the surface of J-CDs. These groups can effectively inhibit the formation of peroxides during high-temperature oxidation of oils by donating hydrogen atoms to stabilize free radicals (Settaluri et al., 2023). Changes in the PAV value of sunflower seed oil were investigated, as illustrated in Fig. 7c. The PAV reflects the content of secondary oxidation products, such as aldehydes, ketones, and quinones, in oils and fats, as well as the extent of oxidation. Higher PAV values indicate more severe oxidation of oils and fats (Karami et al., 2020; Tran et al., 2022). In this study, the PAV values of the oil samples were found to have increased at 170 °C, indicating that high temperature promoted the formation of secondary oxidation products, likely due to the decomposition of hydroperoxides into aldehyde compounds. However, the PAV of the oil samples supplemented with J-CDs were consistently lower than those of the control group. As the addition of J-CDs increased from 0% to 0.06%, the PAV value decreased from 128 to 102, demonstrating that J-CDs can effectively inhibit the formation of secondary oxidation products during lipid oxidation. This further confirms that the J-CDs have a significant effect in delaying oxidation.

Finally, as shown in Fig. 7d, different amounts of the J-CDs had varying effects on the total oxidation value of sunflower oil. As the addition of the J-CDs increased from 0% to 0.06%, the total oxidation value of sunflower oil decreased from 140 to 110, indicating that J-CDs can effectively delay the oxidation process. As previously discussed, the addition of 0.06% J-CDs appears to be the optimal concentration for delaying lipid oxidation. Beyond this concentration, the TOTOX no longer decreased significantly, likely due to excessive J-CDs undergoing polymerization, which shielded the catalytic centers and reduced the antioxidant efficiency. In summary, 0.06% J-CDs can effectively inhibit the oxidation of sunflower oil under simulated high-temperature conditions.

During the frying process, oxidative by-products such as ketones, hydroperoxides, hydroxides, and conjugated dienoic acids accumulate, gradually shifting the color of the oil from light yellow to dark brown. This color change not only affects the visual appearance of the oil but also indicates a transformation in its internal properties (Songohoutou et al., 2023). As shown in Table 1 , with an increase in frying time, the L∗ and b∗ values of all samples gradually decreased, indicating a reduction in the brightness and yellow hue of the oils during the frying process. An increase in the a∗ value indicated that during the frying process, the brightness and yellowness of the oil decreased, whereas its redness increased. This change was likely due to the byproducts generated from the accelerated oxidation at high temperatures. In contrast, the a∗ values exhibited a gradual increase, whereas the b∗ and L∗ values demonstrated a progressive decrease when the J-CDs-spiked oil samples were compared to the control. This result highlights how antioxidants can effectively reduce the color change of oil during frying. Remarkably, the most efficient concentration of J-CDs to prevent oil oxidation was 0.06%. The J-CD-equipped oil samples showed comparatively little color change, suggesting that their capacity to retain the oil color was higher. In summary, owing to their unique characteristics, J-CDs demonstrated significant potential in preventing oil oxidation. This holds considerable value for optimizing the frying process, enhancing food quality, and extending the shelf-life of edible oils.

GC-MS was employed to examine the fatty acid profile of sunflower oil following the incorporation of J-CDs, enabling the determination of the degree of oil oxidation. As shown in Fig. 8 , the major fatty acids changed with different heating times and amounts of J-CDs added. Remarkably, behenic acid (C22:0), linoleic acid (C18:2), linolenic acid (C18:3), and palmitic acid (C16:0) levels decreased in their concentrations in sunflower oil. Stearic acid (C18:0) and oleic acid (C18:1) levels increased with the length of the high-temperature oxidation period. This trend can be explained by the high-temperature oxidation process, which breaks down the double bonds in unsaturated fatty acids, resulting in their progressive transformation into saturated forms. Furthermore, as the concentration of J-CDs increased from 0 to 0.08%, the content of linoleic acid (C18:2) decreased and that of oleic acid (C18:1) increased, suggesting that J-CDs can mitigate the oxidation level of sunflower oil.

In this study, we have examined the antioxidant properties of J-CDs and their effects on oil oxidation. J-CDs exhibited significant scavenging activities against DPPH free radicals (0–64 μg/mL) and ABTS free radicals (0–20 μg/mL), with EC50 values of 29.07 μg/mL and 8.13 μg/mL, respectively. These findings suggest that the antioxidant mechanisms of J-CDs may involve electron transfer to free radicals or hydrogen atom donation from the surface hydrogen-donating functional groups. In the experiments, the addition of J-CDs to sunflower oil resulted in a decrease in the AV, PV, PAV and TOTOX. Additionally, the unsaturated fatty acid content decreased, whereas the saturated fatty acid content increased, indicating that J-CDs possess potent antioxidant capabilities and can effectively delay oil oxidation. As the concentration of J-CDs increased, the inhibitory effect on sunflower oil oxidation became more pronounced. Therefore, J-CDs show promise as potential replacements for synthetic antioxidants and can enhance the oxidative stability of oils.

Yanfei Qin: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Hongqin Chen: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yuanlin Li: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Data curation. Yongguang Huang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology.

The support of the current project was provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 32060534 and 32360571), Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (Natural Science) (Grant No. ZK [2023] 118, 166; ZK [2024] Major Project 016), Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology Support Project (Grant No. [2023] 343), Guizhou Provincial Department of Information Industry Project (Grant No. [2020]198), Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Guizhou Province (Qianke Zhongyindi [2019]4019), General Horizontal Project (Grant No. K22-0115-014, Qianrenlingfa [2020]8) and Guizhou University Natural Science Special Scientific Research Fund Project (X2021327).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Zaichun Xu from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM analysis.
